content:
# Judges Chapter 8 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), c. 1008 CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/hebrew-OT/JDG_page_276.jpg, JDG_page_277.jpg

### Gideon's Victory and Downfall

1. Then the people of Ephraim angrily confronted Gideon. "Why did you treat us like this?" they demanded. "Why didn't you send for us when you first went out to fight the Midianites?"

2. But Gideon replied, "What have I accomplished compared to you? Aren't even the leftover grapes of Ephraim better than the entire grape harvest of my little clan of Abiezer?

3. God gave you victory over Oreb and Zeeb, the commanders of the Midianite army. What have I done compared to that?" When the men of Ephraim heard Gideon's answer, their anger cooled.

4. Gideon then crossed the Jordan River with his 300 men, and though they were exhausted, they continued to chase the enemy.

5. Gideon said to the people of Succoth, "Please give my warriors some food. They are very tired, and I am chasing Zebah and Zalmunna, the kings of Midian."

6. But the officials of Succoth replied, "Catch Zebah and Zalmunna first, and then we'll feed your army."

7. Gideon replied, "After the LORD hands Zebah and Zalmunna over to me, I will come back and tear your flesh with the thorns and briers of the wilderness!"

8. From there Gideon went up to Penuel and made the same request, but he got the same answer.

9. So he said to the people of Penuel, "When I return victorious, I will tear down this tower!"

10. By this time Zebah and Zalmunna were in Karkor with about 15,000 warriors — all that remained of the armies of the east. Already 120,000 of their warriors had been killed.

11. Gideon circled around by the caravan route east of Nobah and Jogbehah, and he took the Midianite army by surprise.

12. Zebah and Zalmunna, the two Midianite kings, fled, but Gideon chased them down and captured them, and their army scattered in panic.

13. After this, Gideon son of Joash returned from the battle by way of the Pass of Heres.

14. He captured a young man from Succoth and demanded that he write down the names of all the seventy-seven officials and elders of the town.

15. Then Gideon went to the men of Succoth and said, "Here are Zebah and Zalmunna. You taunted me, saying, 'Catch Zebah and Zalmunna first, and then we'll feed your exhausted army.'"

16. Then Gideon took the elders of the town and punished them with thorns and briers from the wilderness.

17. He also tore down the tower of Penuel and killed the men of the town.

18. Then Gideon asked Zebah and Zalmunna, "The men you killed at Tabor — what were they like?" They replied, "They looked like you — each one had the appearance of a prince."

19. "Those were my brothers — my own mother's sons!" Gideon replied. "As surely as the LORD lives, I would not kill you if you had spared their lives."

20. Turning to Jether, his oldest son, he said, "Kill them!" But Jether was only a boy, and he was too afraid to draw his sword.

21. Then Zebah and Zalmunna said to Gideon, "Be a man! Kill us yourself!" So Gideon killed them both and took the crescent-shaped ornaments from the necks of their camels.

22. Then the Israelites said to Gideon, "Be our ruler! You and your son and your grandson. For you have rescued us from Midian."

23. But Gideon replied, "I will not rule over you, nor will my son. The LORD will rule over you!"

24. However, Gideon said, "I do have one request — that each of you give me an earring from the plunder you collected." (The enemy wore gold earrings because they were Ishmaelites.)

25. "Gladly!" they replied. They spread out a garment, and each one threw in a gold earring from his plunder.

26. The weight of the gold earrings came to forty-three pounds of gold, not counting the royal ornaments, the pendants, the purple clothing worn by the kings of Midian, or the chains around the camels' necks.

27. Gideon made a sacred ephod from the gold and put it in Ophrah, his hometown. But all Israel prostituted themselves by worshiping it, and it became a trap for Gideon and his family.

28. That is how Midian was subdued by Israel, and the Midianites never again troubled Israel. And there was peace in the land for forty years — as long as Gideon lived.

29. Then Gideon son of Joash returned home.

30. He had seventy sons, for he had many wives.

31. He also had a concubine in Shechem, who bore him a son, whom he named Abimelech.

32. Gideon son of Joash died at a ripe old age and was buried in the tomb of his father Joash in Ophrah of the Abiezrites.

33. As soon as Gideon died, the Israelites prostituted themselves again by worshiping the Baals. They set up Baal-berith as their god.

34. They forgot the LORD their God, who had rescued them from all their enemies on every side.

35. Nor did they show any loyalty to the family of Gideon (also called Jerubbaal) despite all the good he had done for Israel.

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

**Gideon's tragedy:** Chapter 8 is the hinge of the Gideon story. He begins as a hero and ends as something much more complicated. He refuses the crown ("The LORD will rule over you") but then acts like a king — demanding tribute, making a priestly garment, taking many wives, and naming a son "My Father Is King." The narrator doesn't editorialize; the facts speak.

**The ephod (v.27):** The gold ephod is the most troubling detail. An ephod was a priestly vestment used for seeking divine guidance. Made from 43 pounds of gold plundered from Midian, Gideon's ephod becomes an idol. The very man who tore down Baal's altar creates a new object of false worship in its place. The cycle repeats within a single lifetime.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
